metadata.title: Quick-fire: Honest feedback under different gradients
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Companion to qf-truth-001 (in-the-moment lies), qf-truth-003 (modality variation), qf-truth-005 (past-misrepresentations reactivated). This round focuses on opinion/evaluative honesty rather than fact-honesty — giving and receiving feedback across power-asymmetric contexts: peer to peer, upward (subordinate giving manager feedback), parental (child asking about you), professional advice (doctor/expert telling you something you don't want to hear), partner (intimate-context honesty about the relationship), friend (creative work). Tests whether the user's honesty norm differs based on who's asking and what's being evaluated.
metadata.domain_anchor: Bies & Shapiro 1987 on procedural fairness in interpersonal feedback; Sedikides & Strube self-evaluation maintenance; Edmondson on psychological safety and upward honesty in organizations.

items.prompt: A friend has shown you their creative project (a draft of something they care about — book, music, art). They ask what you think. You think it's middling.

options.text: Tell them honestly, with specifics: 'I think these parts work; these parts I'd rework.'
Focus on what works; leave the weaknesses for them to discover. 'I really liked X.'

items.prompt: Your romantic partner asks: 'Are you happy with how we are right now?' You're not entirely — there are real things to talk about, but they're not at a crisis point.

options.text: Say what you actually think, even though the conversation will be hard: 'Honestly, there are a couple of things I want to talk about.'
Reassure now, raise things later when there's a better moment: 'Yes, basically.'

items.prompt: Your manager asks for upward feedback on their leadership. You have substantive concerns about how decisions get made on your team — concerns they'd find uncomfortable to hear.

options.text: Share the substantive concerns clearly, with specifics. The asking was sincere; honesty is the right response.
Give measured, low-friction feedback. Honest upward feedback under power asymmetry is high-risk; pick a small concrete thing.

items.prompt: Your child (or younger family member) asks an honest question about something embarrassing from your past — a known family story but with you holding the inside view.

options.text: Tell them the inside view, age-appropriately. The asking was sincere and the relationship benefits from real honesty about real things.
Give them the family-story version. The inside view is yours; they can earn it when they're older.

items.prompt: Your doctor (or other professional you trust) gives you a diagnosis or assessment you don't agree with. They've been thorough but you think they're wrong.

options.text: Push back directly: 'I hear you, but I disagree. Here's why...' Engage with the diagnosis as something to discuss.
Accept it in the meeting; get a second opinion separately. The conflict isn't useful in the room.

items.prompt: You're nominating someone for an award. The strongest candidate by the explicit criteria is someone you don't personally like. There's also a person you like who's almost-but-not-quite as strong.

options.text: Nominate the strongest candidate. Personal feelings shouldn't override the criteria you were given.
Nominate the person you like — they meet the bar, and the soft-criteria of fit-and-collaboration count too.
